cases.title: Component kind is accepted as a valid plugin manifest discriminator value
preconditions: Roubo server is running
plugin-manager is active
A minimal roubo-plugin.yaml with kind: component is present in ~/.roubo/plugins/test-component/

steps.instruction: Place a roubo-plugin.yaml at ~/.roubo/plugins/test-component/roubo-plugin.yaml with fields: name, version, kind: component, contractVersion: 1, permissions: { processes: true }
Trigger plugin discovery by restarting the Roubo server or calling the plugin-manager reload path

observations.expected: The manifest is parsed without a validation error
The plugin appears in the active plugin list with kind equal to component

steps.instruction: Inspect the plugin-manager internal state for the loaded plugin entry

observations.expected: The entry carries kind: component and not kind: integration

cases.title: SDK ComponentContract declarative path: translate returns a valid ProvisionDescriptor
preconditions: plugin-sdk is available
A test plugin calls defineComponentPlugin() and registers a translate() implementation

steps.instruction: Invoke component.translate({ config: { image: 'postgres:15', port: 5432 }, context: { projectId: 'p1', benchId: 1, componentName: 'db', workspacePath: '/tmp/ws', ports: { db: 5432 }, env: {} } }) over the JSON-RPC stdio transport

observations.expected: The response is a ProvisionDescriptor with kind: docker and all required fields (composeFile, service)
The descriptor contains a schemaVersion field matching the host's supported descriptorSchemaVersion

steps.instruction: Verify the LifecycleEngine caches the returned descriptor for benchId 1 / componentName db

observations.expected: A second start call for the same bench uses the cached descriptor and does not call translate again

cases.title: SDK ComponentContract imperative path: start/stop/health/cleanup hooks are dispatched correctly
preconditions: An imperative-mode component plugin is registered via defineComponentPlugin() with start, stop, health, and cleanup hooks (no translate)
contractVersion is set to 1 in the manifest
plugin-manager has discovered and spawned the plugin

steps.instruction: Call component.start(BenchContext) over JSON-RPC

observations.expected: The plugin's start hook is invoked with the correct BenchContext shape
The hook calls host.process.start or a broker method; the host records the process id in the ResourceOwnershipLedger

steps.instruction: Call component.health(BenchContext) over JSON-RPC

observations.expected: Returns a ComponentStatus object with a non-empty status field

steps.instruction: Call component.stop(BenchContext) over JSON-RPC

observations.expected: The plugin's stop hook is invoked; the owned process is stopped via the broker
ComponentStatus transitions to stopped

steps.instruction: Call component.cleanup(BenchContext) over JSON-RPC

observations.expected: The cleanup hook runs without error and the ledger entry for this bench is cleared

cases.title: roubo.yaml component binding is parsed and resolves a plugin by id with opaque config block
preconditions: A roubo.yaml exists with a components section that binds a component name to a plugin id
The referenced plugin is installed and its manifest is valid

steps.instruction: Parse the roubo.yaml containing: components: { cache: { plugin: { id: 'com.example.redis' }, config: { maxMemory: '128m' } } }

observations.expected: The parser produces a ComponentBinding with plugin.id equal to com.example.redis and config equal to { maxMemory: '128m' }
No validation error is raised for the opaque config block; it is passed through untouched to the plugin

steps.instruction: Start a bench that includes the cache component

observations.expected: ComponentPluginRegistry.resolveBinding returns the live JSON-RPC connection for com.example.redis
The config block { maxMemory: '128m' } is forwarded to translate() or start() as the config argument

cases.title: Component plugin config block is validated against the plugin's own configSchema
preconditions: A component plugin declares a configSchema in its manifest (JSON Schema format)
The plugin is installed and loaded

steps.instruction: Supply a roubo.yaml config block that satisfies the plugin's configSchema (e.g., required field maxMemory is present)

observations.expected: Bench start proceeds without a config validation error

steps.instruction: Supply a roubo.yaml config block that violates the plugin's configSchema (e.g., maxMemory is missing)

observations.expected: Bench start is rejected with an error that names the failing component and the violated schema constraint
No processes or containers are started; the bench does not reach starting state

cases.title: Local drop-in component plugin in ~/.roubo/plugins is discovered, validated, spawned, and supervised
preconditions: ~/.roubo/plugins/my-redis/ directory exists with a valid roubo-plugin.yaml (kind: component) and an index.js entry point
Roubo server is not yet running (cold start)

steps.instruction: Start the Roubo server

observations.expected: plugin-manager discovers my-redis in the drop-in directory
The manifest is validated: kind is component, permissions are present, HOST_API_VERSION semver check passes
The plugin process is spawned via stdio JSON-RPC and appears in the supervised plugin list

observations.expected: plugin-manager detects the exit and fires onComponentPluginPreRestart
bench-manager reads the ledger and stops any owned processes/containers before restart

cases.title: Component plugin manifest declares ports and docker permission categories

steps.instruction: Write a manifest with permissions: { network: true, processes: true, ports: true, docker: true } and load it via plugin-manager

observations.expected: The manifest passes schema validation with no unknown-field error for ports or docker
The plugin's declared categories list includes network, processes, ports, and docker

steps.instruction: Fetch GET /api/plugins/my-redis-plugin/consent

observations.expected: The response body contains a declared object listing network, processes, ports, and docker

cases.title: host.capability.query returns available: true for a supported broker method and available: false for an unknown method
preconditions: An imperative component plugin is running and connected via JSON-RPC

steps.instruction: From inside the plugin's start hook, call host.capability.query({ method: 'host.docker.composeUp' })

observations.expected: Response is { available: true, introducedIn: '<version string>' }

steps.instruction: From inside the plugin's start hook, call host.capability.query({ method: 'host.docker.nonExistentMethod' })

observations.expected: Response is { available: false } with no introducedIn field

cases.title: Invoking a host-RPC method after capability.query returns false produces a clear error, not a bench crash
preconditions: An imperative component plugin is connected
The host is running a version that does not support host.docker.assignContainer (simulated by patching the broker)

steps.instruction: Plugin calls host.capability.query({ method: 'host.docker.assignContainer' }) and receives { available: false }

observations.expected: Query completes without error and the plugin receives the false availability signal

steps.instruction: Plugin proceeds to call host.docker.assignContainer despite the false availability signal

observations.expected: The broker returns a JSON-RPC error response with a descriptive message naming the missing method and the minimum host version that supports it
The bench remains in its pre-call state; no crash, no orphaned containers
The error is surfaced in ComponentStatus.error and the component transitions to error state, not stopped state

cases.title: Existing integration plugins continue to load and operate after the component kind is introduced

No manifest-validation error is reported for the integration plugin

steps.instruction: Exercise the integration plugin's normal RPC flow (e.g., list issues via the integration)

observations.expected: The integration plugin responds correctly; behaviour is identical to before the update
No interference from the component-kind extensions is observable

cases.title: A component plugin whose HOST_API_VERSION semver requirement is not met fails gracefully at load time

cases.title: Manifest with an unknown kind value is rejected at validation time

cases.title: A plugin implementing both translate and imperative hooks is rejected at load time

steps.instruction: Spawn the plugin process and allow plugin-manager to perform post-spawn method introspection

observations.expected: plugin-manager detects that both translate and start are registered and emits a contract-violation error

cases.title: A declarative plugin returning a ProvisionDescriptor with an unsupported schemaVersion is rejected before execution

cases.title: ProvisionDescriptor oneshot kind reaches completed terminal status on exit-code 0
preconditions: A declarative plugin returns a ProvisionDescriptor with kind: oneshot, command: echo done, timeoutMs: 5000

cases.title: Plugin process crash during start triggers ledger-based cleanup with no orphaned containers

cases.title: Plugin start latency overhead over built-in component is within +500ms p95

observations.expected: The plugin p95 start time is no more than 500ms higher than the built-in p95 start time

cases.title: E2E: plugin author scaffolds, consumer installs, and runs a third-party component plugin end to end

steps.instruction: Consumer: stop the bench via DELETE /api/projects/:projectId/benches/:id

observations.expected: cache component transitions to stopping then stopped

cases.title: ProvisionDescriptor union declares a oneshot kind with the required fields
preconditions: The ProvisionDescriptor type definition is available in shared/ (architecture data-model section)
The architecture document and PRD CP-FR-022 are the authoritative contract source

steps.instruction: Locate the ProvisionDescriptor discriminated union in shared/ (or its authoritative schema definition in the architecture data-model table)
Verify the union includes a member with kind 'oneshot' carrying command (string), and optional env, cwd, dependsOn, and timeoutMs, alongside the docker and process members

observations.expected: The oneshot member is present as a distinct branch of the discriminated union, not as a subtype of process or docker
command is a required string field on the oneshot member
env, cwd, dependsOn, and timeoutMs are all optional fields on the oneshot member, matching the architecture data-model shape exactly
The kind discriminant is the literal string 'oneshot', allowing standard TypeScript narrowing without any runtime heuristics

steps.instruction: Confirm the oneshot member carries schemaVersion at the envelope level (inherited from the ProvisionDescriptor base), so the host LifecycleEngine can validate and reject mismatched schema versions

observations.expected: schemaVersion appears at the top level of every ProvisionDescriptor variant, including oneshot, per the architecture data-model shape

cases.title: host.process.run is defined as a distinct blocking run-to-completion broker method separate from host.process.start
preconditions: The HostComponentBroker RPC surface definition is available in the architecture interfaces/contracts section
The distinction between start (long-lived) and run (blocking one-shot) is the authoritative contract boundary

steps.instruction: Locate the HostComponentBroker method list in the architecture document (component plugin -> HostComponentBroker section)
Verify host.process.run is listed as a separate named method that accepts id, command, args, env, cwd, timeoutMs and returns exitCode

observations.expected: host.process.run appears as an explicitly named entry in the broker surface, distinct from host.process.start
The return type is exitCode (a scalar result), contrasting with host.process.start which returns pid (a handle to a resident process)
timeoutMs is a parameter on host.process.run and not on host.process.start, reflecting that only one-shot processes require a hung-process bound

steps.instruction: Confirm host.process.run is described as blocking (the caller awaits the process exit) while host.process.start is described as long-lived (the caller receives a pid and must query or stop separately)

observations.expected: The architecture labels host.process.run as 'blocking run-to-completion' and host.process.start as 'long-lived', making the semantic contract unambiguous

steps.instruction: Confirm both methods share the same broker transport (JSON-RPC over stdio via HostComponentBroker) so a plugin author can call either without switching API layers

observations.expected: Both host.process.start and host.process.run are listed under the same HostComponentBroker surface and use the same JSON-RPC/stdio transport

cases.title: ComponentStatus includes a completed terminal state distinct from stopped and error
preconditions: The ComponentStatus data-model entry is available in the architecture document
The PRD deltas / Supersedes table (CP-FR-014 / CP-FR-022 row) is the authoritative change record

steps.instruction: Locate the ComponentStatus entity definition in the architecture data-model table
Verify the status field of ComponentStatus enumerates completed as a distinct value alongside stopped, starting, running, error, and stopping

observations.expected: completed appears as a named literal in the status union, not as an alias for stopped or a subcase of error
The full enum is stopped, starting, running, error, stopping, completed, matching the architecture data-model table exactly

steps.instruction: Confirm that completed is classified as a terminal state (no further automatic transitions expected) and that the architecture explicitly distinguishes it from stopped (an idle/cleared state) and error (a failed-exit state)

observations.expected: The architecture PRD-delta note for CP-FR-014/CP-FR-022 states that a one-shot deploy that exits 0 is neither stopped (idle) nor error, so a distinct terminal state keeps the status surface honest
No architecture text equates completed with stopped or error; the states are semantically disjoint

steps.instruction: Verify that the completed state is reachable only via a successful (exit 0) one-shot lifecycle path, preserving the integrity of the error and stopped states for their existing meanings

observations.expected: The architecture CP-FR-022 sequence flow describes host.process.run resolving with an exit code and the plugin reporting ComponentStatus.status = completed for a successful deploy, implying non-zero exit maps to error, not completed

cases.title: A deploy can be expressed declaratively as a oneshot descriptor without requiring a new plugin kind
preconditions: The ProvisionDescriptor union and the ComponentContract translate path are defined in the architecture
CP-FR-022 states the deploy capability must fit the existing kind boundary without redesign

steps.instruction: Confirm that the ProvisionDescriptor union already includes oneshot as a first-class member and that no new discriminator value outside docker, process, oneshot is required to represent a deploy lifecycle

observations.expected: The three-member union (docker, process, oneshot) is sufficient to represent a Clasp deploy without adding a fourth member

steps.instruction: Trace the declarative path for a hypothetical Clasp deploy: a plugin implementing translate(config) returns a oneshot descriptor with command 'clasp push' and a timeoutMs, and confirm the LifecycleEngine can drive it without any engine changes

observations.expected: The LifecycleEngine generic executor handles the oneshot descriptor branch (validate schemaVersion, record ownership in the ResourceOwnershipLedger, call host.process.run, write ComponentStatus.completed) purely from the existing descriptor contract
No engine code path is gated on a plugin-specific kind string beyond the descriptor's own kind discriminant

steps.instruction: Confirm the plugin manifest kind discriminator remains component (not a new deploy kind) for a deploy plugin using the oneshot descriptor

observations.expected: The manifest kind 'component' is unchanged; the oneshot lifecycle variation is expressed in the ProvisionDescriptor, not by introducing a new manifest kind
plugin-manager discovers, validates, spawns, and supervises a deploy plugin identically to any other component plugin, requiring no plugin-manager changes

cases.title: A deploy can be expressed imperatively via the broker escape hatch without requiring a new plugin kind
preconditions: The imperative hook path (start/stop/health/cleanup) and the HostComponentBroker are defined in the architecture
CP-FR-022 states the architecture must accommodate deploy via either the declarative or the imperative path

steps.instruction: Confirm that the ComponentContract imperative hook set (component.start, component.stop, component.health, component.cleanup) is available to a deploy plugin without modification to the SDK contract

observations.expected: The ComponentContract already includes the imperative hook signatures; no new hook names or SDK extension is required to express a deploy lifecycle

steps.instruction: Trace the imperative escape-hatch path for a hypothetical multi-step Clasp deploy (OAuth token check, project selection, push): the plugin's start hook calls host.process.run for each step in sequence and the architecture accommodates this without a new broker method

observations.expected: host.process.run is already in the broker surface and is sufficient to model each sub-step of a multi-phase deploy; no new broker method is required
The plugin may call host.process.run multiple times sequentially inside component.start; the broker imposes no constraint on call count within a lifecycle phase

steps.instruction: Confirm that on successful completion the plugin calls host.component.reportStatus with status 'completed' and the architecture treats this as a valid terminal transition for a component plugin using the imperative path

observations.expected: host.component.reportStatus is available to imperative-path plugins and accepts the completed status value, per the architecture sequence flow for CP-FR-022

steps.instruction: Confirm the plugin manifest kind remains component and plugin-manager lifecycle is unchanged for an imperative deploy plugin

observations.expected: An imperative deploy plugin registers kind 'component' in its manifest, implementing the hook contract rather than translate; no additional manifest field or new kind value is introduced

cases.title: A hung one-shot lifecycle is bounded by timeoutMs on host.process.run so it cannot block the plugin-manager restart budget
preconditions: The host.process.run signature includes timeoutMs as a parameter per the architecture broker surface
The plugin-manager restart budget is 3 restarts within 5 minutes per the architecture operational-concerns section

steps.instruction: Confirm that host.process.run accepts a timeoutMs parameter and the architecture specifies the host enforces this bound (the process is killed by the host, not the plugin)

observations.expected: timeoutMs is listed as a parameter on host.process.run in the architecture broker surface table
The architecture operational-concerns section notes that a hung one-shot is bounded by timeoutMs so a stuck deploy does not block the plugin-manager restart budget

steps.instruction: Confirm that when timeoutMs elapses without process exit, the expected host behavior results in an error-mapped outcome (not a completed outcome), so the ComponentStatus transitions to error rather than completed

observations.expected: A timeout expiry on host.process.run does not produce exitCode 0 and therefore must not result in ComponentStatus.status = completed; the contract implies a non-zero or error result

steps.instruction: Confirm that a plugin author can set timeoutMs on the oneshot ProvisionDescriptor (declarative path) and on the host.process.run call (imperative path), covering both entry points

observations.expected: The oneshot descriptor includes timeoutMs as an optional field, allowing the LifecycleEngine to forward it to host.process.run automatically on the declarative path
The imperative hook path also accepts timeoutMs directly on host.process.run, giving the plugin author the same bound without requiring the declarative descriptor

steps.instruction: Confirm the architecture does not rely on the plugin itself to enforce the timeout, ensuring the host-owns model preserves cleanup integrity even when the plugin process is also unresponsive

observations.expected: The host (HostComponentBroker / LifecycleEngine) is solely responsible for enforcing the timeoutMs bound, consistent with the host-owns-lifecycle decision rationale

cases.title: The broker is the single privileged choke-point for both declarative and imperative deploy paths, preserving enforcement and audit attach points
preconditions: The HostComponentBroker design rationale is documented in the architecture security and decision sections
CP-FR-022 requires a deploy to be addable without redesign, which includes the v2 audit/enforcement layer

steps.instruction: Confirm the architecture states that both the declarative LifecycleEngine and the imperative hooks funnel every privileged operation through HostComponentBroker with no bypass path

observations.expected: The architecture security section states that enforcement and audit attach at exactly one layer (the broker), regardless of whether the plugin uses the declarative or imperative path

steps.instruction: Confirm that a deploy plugin using host.process.run would, in v2, have its calls intercepted by the PermissionEnforcer at the broker layer without any deploy-specific code in PermissionEnforcer

observations.expected: PermissionEnforcer intercepts all broker calls uniformly; host.process.run is a broker method and therefore automatically subject to v2 enforcement without a new enforcement rule
The AuditLog would record host.process.run calls from a deploy plugin identically to any other broker method, requiring no audit-schema change

steps.instruction: Confirm the ResourceOwnershipLedger records any process spawned via host.process.run (even for a one-shot), ensuring cleanup on crash covers a deploy plugin without a ledger schema change

observations.expected: The ResourceOwnershipLedger maps (pluginId, benchId) to processIds and composeProjects; host.process.run adds a processId to this map for the duration of the run, so crash cleanup via onComponentPluginPreRestart covers it

cases.title: Adding a deploy plugin requires zero changes to core (bench-manager, LifecycleEngine, plugin-manager, broker surface)
preconditions: The architecture documents the full set of components that must remain unchanged when a new component plugin is added
CP-US-012 states deploy must be addable without redesign; CP-NFR-006 requires zero component-type literals in core

steps.instruction: Enumerate every core module that would need to change to support a net-new long-running component type today (bench-manager type dispatch, config-schema enum), and confirm the architecture has removed all such dispatch sites as part of the component-plugins feature

observations.expected: The architecture states that all type-dispatch is removed from bench-manager (launchComponent / stopComponent / reconcile / refreshComponentStatuses / assignContainer / teardown branches), leaving no site that would need updating for oneshot

steps.instruction: Confirm that the LifecycleEngine already handles the oneshot descriptor branch and that adding a deploy plugin does not require adding a new engine branch

observations.expected: The LifecycleEngine is a generic executor over the ProvisionDescriptor union; because oneshot is already a union member, the engine handles it without modification at plugin-install time

steps.instruction: Confirm that the broker surface (host.process.run, host.component.reportStatus) required by a deploy plugin is already present in the v1 broker definition, so no broker extension is needed when the deploy plugin is written

observations.expected: All methods a deploy plugin would call (host.process.run, host.component.reportStatus, host.capability.query) are enumerated in the v1 HostComponentBroker surface; the deploy plugin ships as a pure addition with no broker changes

steps.instruction: Confirm that the CP-NFR-006 ComponentTypeKnowledgeGuard CI check would continue to pass after adding a deploy plugin, because the plugin ships outside core and introduces no component-type literals in bench-manager or shared/

observations.expected: A deploy plugin is authored as a standalone package (bundled or user-installed in ~/.roubo/plugins); it does not modify bench-manager, LifecycleEngine, or HostComponentBroker, so the CI guard stays green
